coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of a novel nitrogen-doped carbon dot by microwave-assisted carbonization method and its applications as selective probes for optical pH (acidity) sensing in aqueous/nonaqueous media, determination of nitrate/nitrite, and optical recognition of NOX gas
coredata.dc:creator: Doroodmand, Mohammad Mahdi
Askari, Mohsen
coredata.dc:description: A novel nitrogen-doped carbon dot (N-CD) was synthesized via carbonization of citric acid in the presence of triethylenetetramine as a nitrogen source. The average size of the N-doped CDs and also the quantum yield of the synthesized N-doped CDs were both estimated to be 9 ± 2 nm and 39.5%, respectively. The applications of the synthesized carbon nanostructure as a high quantum yield fluorescence probe were initially adopted in the fabrication of a novel optical pH (acidity) sensor in both aqueous and nonaqueous environments. Two optimum dynamic intervals were obtained with the ranges of1.5–5.0 and 7.0–10.0. for the fabricated pH sensor with a standard deviation of 0.09 pH (n = 4). The quantity of HClO4 inside acetic acid was determined as the degree of acidity with a linear range between 1.0 and 4.0%. Determination of nitrate (NO3 −) and nitrite (NO2 −) based on the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs was also evaluated in detail. The linear ranges for NO2 − and NO3 − species were estimated to be from 1 × 10−7to 7.5 × 10−5 and from 2.5 × 10−6 to 7.5 × 10−4 mol L−1, respectively with RSD of 3.69% (n = 5) for NO2 − and 3.54% (n = 5) for NO3 −. The LODs (X+3Sb) for both NO2 − and NO3 − were estimated to be 2.5 × 10−8 and 7.5 × 10−7 mol L−1, respectively. The synthesized N-CDs were also applicable for NOX recognition in the gaseous form at part per thousand (ppt) levels with linear ranges of 3.77–36.51 and 27.67–43.77 ppt, LOD (X+3Sb) of 1.41 ppt (n = 4) and RSD of 4.37% (n = 5). The reliability of these methods was also evaluated via the analyses of different forms of gaseous, water and rumen samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acidity
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Quantum yield
Fluorescence probe
Quenching process

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calibration curve and figures of merit
Calibration curve and figures of merit

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the designed system during the fluorescence measuring of NOX.
Typical absorption, excitation and normalized fluorescence spectra of C-Dots.
A) HRSEM, B) HRTEM photographs of C- Dots, and C) Particle size analyzer.
Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized C- Dots at the excitation.

Fluorescence spectra of pH including in the aqueous environment in the ranges: A) 7.0–10.0 and B) 1.5–5.0, inset: calibration curves at room temperature. C) Fluorescence spectra in acetic acid solution at different percentages of HClO4 at room temperature.

A) Fluorescence spectra of C-Dots in the presence and absence of NO2 − (2.0 × 10−5 mol L−1), B) The effect of pH on the quenching of the fluorescence intensity of C- Dots solution in the presence of nitrite (2.0 × 10−5 mol L−1) with a time duration of 6. 0 min (n = 4) measured at room temperature, excitation and emission slit of 10 nm, and C) effect of temperature on the quenching of fluorescence of C- Dots. At excitation and emission slit of 10 nm, the ionic strength of 0.5 mol L−1 and pH 2.0.

A, B) The fluorescence spectra and C, D) calibration curve of NO2 − and NO3 − standard solutions at room temperature and 0.75 mol L−1 ionic strength of 0.75 mol L−1 ions and at 350 nm excitation wavelength.
Interferences study for determination of NO2 − and NO3 −.
A, B) Fluorescence spectra and C, D) calibration curve of NOx standard solutions at room temperature, pH 2.0, and excitation and emission slits of 10 nm.
Real sample analysis for determination of pH in aqueous solutions.

row.entry: pH after addition of 10.0 μL HCl (0.5 mol L-1)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Real sample analysis for measuring acidity in nonaqueous solution.

row.entry: Reversible pH sensing over wide pH range
Electrochemical method (CV) by Carbon fiber microelectrode
Compression between different methods of detection of NO2 − and NO3 −

head.ce:title: Synthesis of a novel nitrogen-doped carbon dot by microwave-assisted carbonization method and its applications as selective probes for optical pH (acidity) sensing in aqueous/nonaqueous media, determination of nitrate/nitrite, and optical recognition of NOX gas

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shiraz University, Shiraz 71454, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel nitrogen-doped carbon dot (N-CD) was synthesized via carbonization of citric acid in the presence of triethylenetetramine as a nitrogen source. The average size of the N-doped CDs and also the quantum yield of the synthesized N-doped CDs were both estimated to be 9 ± 2 nm and 39.5%, respectively. The applications of the synthesized carbon nanostructure as a high quantum yield fluorescence probe were initially adopted in the fabrication of a novel optical pH (acidity) sensor in both aqueous and nonaqueous environments. Two optimum dynamic intervals were obtained with the ranges of1.5–5.0 and 7.0–10.0. for the fabricated pH sensor with a standard deviation of 0.09 pH (n = 4). The quantity of HClO4 inside acetic acid was determined as the degree of acidity with a linear range between 1.0 and 4.0%. Determination of nitrate (NO3 −) and nitrite (NO2 −) based on the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs was also evaluated in detail. The linear ranges for NO2 − and NO3 − species were estimated to be from 1 × 10−7to 7.5 × 10−5 and from 2.5 × 10−6 to 7.5 × 10−4 mol L−1, respectively with RSD of 3.69% (n = 5) for NO2 − and 3.54% (n = 5) for NO3 −. The LODs (X+3Sb) for both NO2 − and NO3 − were estimated to be 2.5 × 10−8 and 7.5 × 10−7 mol L−1, respectively. The synthesized N-CDs were also applicable for NOX recognition in the gaseous form at part per thousand (ppt) levels with linear ranges of 3.77–36.51 and 27.67–43.77 ppt, LOD (X+3Sb) of 1.41 ppt (n = 4) and RSD of 4.37% (n = 5). The reliability of these methods was also evaluated via the analyses of different forms of gaseous, water and rumen samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (C-Dots) are considered as a new branch of carbon nanostructures with sizes less than 10 nm. This compound was primarily realized during the purification of carbon nanomaterials such as single-walled carbon nanotubes through preparative electrophoresis in 2004 [1]. Compared to the other chemical species such as semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and organic dyes, photoluminescent C-Dots are accepted as a new reagent with unique characteristics such as high aqueous solubility, robust chemical inertness, easy functionalization, high resistance to photobleaching, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [1–3].

Besides the excellent advantages of the C-Dot, this reagent has possessed fluorescence properties with high quantum yield. Moreover, the photoluminescence (PL) from C-Dots can be quenched professionally [2,3]. All these properties indicate the donor/acceptor characteristics of the C-Dots [2,3]. These properties point to the capability of PL C-Dots for various applications such as light energy conversion, fabrication of photovoltaic devices, solar cells, and so on [2,3]. The excellent capability of C-Dots is related to its structure, which often consists of elements such as carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen with quasi-spherical shape [4]. However, doping C-Dots with other elements such as nitrogen and sulfur [5,6] or metal ions (e.g., Co, Gd, Si) [7–9] may majorly promote not only the optical properties of this species [4], but also makes this reagent more suitable for other analytical applications such as acidity (pH) measurement or recognition [10]. During the last decades, due to the various advantages (benefits) of the C-Dot including the ease of synthesis, low cost, high quantum yield, acceptable stability and its capability for different analytical purposes [11,12], this compound has attracted the scientist's attention all over the world.

Acidity recognition in both aqueous and non-aqueous media is considered as one of the most important and common analytical tests, which are achieved in many laboratories. The importance of acidity management is strongly related to the effective role of pH on the chemical and biological properties of different substances. For instance, due to the accompany of ions such as H+ or OH− in the alternative reactions, often the solubility of different inorganic species such as CaCO3 effectively depends on the quantity of acidity (pH) value. Furthermore, pH can affect the rate (kinetic) of chemical reactions. Due to the phenomena such as proton-transfer reactions, factors such as solvent-solute interactions are often affected by the acidic/basic strength [13]. Therefore, pH monitoring is important in the laboratory and industrial processes.

The pH of non-aqueous media is usually measured using a pH-sensitive glass electrode. Its response to pH has been confirmed to be Nernstian in various aqueous and non-aqueous solvents during the estimation of the acid dissociation constants using the glass electrode as a pH-sensor. However, the potential response of the glass electrode in the non-aqueous environments is often very slow so that in some cases, it takes over one hour before the equilibrium potential is reached [14]. It seems that focusing on the PL C-Dots can solve these problems during reliable estimation of the acidity. This information points to the importance of acidity measurement especially in the sophisticated non-aqueous or aqueous/non-aqueous mixed samples during the estimation of analytical figures of merit [15].

Another important application of the synthesized PL C-Dots is its application for simultaneous detection of various species such as nitrate and nitrite. The importance of simultaneous of these two reagents is related to their effective roles in the environmental transformations [16]. In addition, nitrate is considered often as the source of nitrogen during the synthesis of proteins by microorganisms [16]. In spite of this importance, high concentrations of these reagents are seriously toxic and may lead to death by asphyxia [16–19]. According to the report of the “World Health Organization (2010)”, maximum safe limits of nitrate and nitrite in drinking water has been estimated to be 50 and 3 mg L-1, respectively [17]. Therefore, the introduction of a new analytical method for the simultaneous determination of nitrate and nitrite in food, agricultural soil and water samples is important all over the world [16].

From another aspect, determination of the purity of NOx species is essential in different parts of industries, including estimation of the quality and quantity of NOx gas, evaluation of purity percentage (grade) of the NOx-containing cylinders as well as the standardization of NOx. It is also important in the introduction of selective and sensitive methods for the recognition of NOx gas (as a toxic gas) at large levels such as part per thousand (ppt) scales. All these reports signify the strong demand for the introduction of a rapid, sensitive and reliable method for the detection purposes. To achieve this goal, hereby in this study, a novel nitrogen-doped carbon dot (N-CD) is synthesized and adopted for the detection of various analyses such as pH and acidity in aqueous and nonaqueous media, simultaneous determination of NO3 −/NO2 − and NOx gas by fluorescence spectrometry.

Gasses including nitrogen (99.98%) and carbon dioxide (99.0%) were purchased from “Parsbaloon, Iran”. NO2 − stock solution (1.0 mmol L−1) was prepared by dissolving 34.50 ± 0.01 mg sodium nitrite (NaNO2, Merck) in ∼20.0 mL triply-distilled water and transferring into a 500.0-mL volumetric flux. The fresh standard solution of NO2 − was generated via the dilution of the stock solution. NO3 − stock solution (1.0 mmol L−1) was prepared by dissolving 50.55 ± 0.01 mg potassium nitrate (KNO3, Merck) in ∼20.0 mL triply-distilled water and transferring into a 500.0-mL volumetric flux. NO2 − and NO3 − (1.0 mmol L−1) were prepared by dissolving 34.50 ± 0.01 mg sodium nitrite (NaNO2, Merck) and 50.55 ± 0.01 mg potassium nitrate (KNO3, Merck), respectively in ∼20.0 mL triply-distilled water and transferring into a 500.0-mL volumetric flux. A fresh standard solution of NO3 − and NO2 − were generated via dilution of the stock solution. The universal buffer solution was prepared according to a reported procedure [19]. The Analytical grade of citric acid ((HOOCCH2)2C(OH)COOH), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl), acetic acid (CH3COOH), potassium hydroxide (KOH), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium hypochlorite (NaClO4), sodium triphosphate (Na3(PO4)), sodium fluoride (NaF), boric acid (H3BO3) and potassium chloride (KCl) were purchased from Merck company. Copper (Cu) powder metal (purity: 99.9%), cadmium metal (purity: 99.99%) and triethylenetetra amine were related to the Sigma-Aldrich.

Pyrolysis process was done using a domestic microwave (LG-WaveDom (MS2387RS)). A domestic microwave oven (LG-WaveDom (MS2387RS)) with temperature program system was also adopted to synthesize C-Dots. All the fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer LS50B spectrofluorometer equipped with a thermostated cell compartment containing 1.0-cm quartz cuvettes. The excitation wavelengths were selected using the synthesized C-Dots used. Various methods were applied for characterization of the synthesized C-Dots, including Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (FT-IR-8300 Shimadzu), patterned X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8Advance with Cu-Kα, λ = 0.1542 nm), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, model: PHI 5000 Versa), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Zeiss-EM10C-80KV, 300 kV), and high resolution scanning electron microscopy (HRSEM, KYKY-EM3500). All the XRD characterizations and calculations were performed by using “X′ PERT HIGH SCORE” software. A pH meter (Metrohm 827 PHlab swiss made) was used for the accurate pH adjustment. Water bath (model: Polystat cc2) was also used to control the temperature during the study.

To synthesize N-doped C-Dots (N-CDs), 25.0 g citric acid (CA) was dissolved in 400.0 mL triply-distilled water. After that, 5.0 mL triethylenetetramine was dropped into the solution during ∼3 min time intervals, followed by assisting with microwave radiation (power = 650 W) for ∼ 50 min. The synthesized N-CDs were then dried in a desiccator (volume: ∼5 L), followed by scratching the sediment inside the petri dish by a spatula and making powder by an agate mortar and finally closing inside a container (vial). This nanostructure was directly adopted for analytical purposes without the need for any passivation or activation. It should be considered that the time of microwave radiation (50 min) was estimated to support high values production. To produce the lab scale production (rate: ∼35 g h−1), the time of microwave radiation would be ∼10 min and also the amount of water, carbon source (citric acid) and nitrogen source (triethylenetetramine) should be reduced.

For the optical pH sensing, a solution of N-CDs (5.0 mg L−1) was prepared in an aqueous solution via the introduction of 2.5 mg N-CDs into a 500.0-mL volumetric flask, followed by dilution using triply-distilled water and controlling the pH by concentrated HCl and NaOH. The pH value was finally measured using a pH sensitive glass electrode as the standard pH meter. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was also followed as the selective pH probe.

For the optical acidity sensing in the non-aqueous medium, a solution of N-CDs (5.0 mg L−1) was prepared by the introduction of 2.5 mg N-CDs into a 500.0 mL-volumetric flask, and dilution to the mark with acetic acid was as a solvent. The acidity of the non-aqueous solution was also controlled using perchloric acid (HClO4). For detection of the percentage of perchloric acid to identify the percentage of perchloric acid) as the order of acidity, a solution of C-Dots in acetic acid (solvent) was prepared. Then different percentages of HClO4 (solute) were added, and changes in fluorescence intensities were followed up.

The synthesized N-CDs were used as fluorophore probes. For this purpose, a stock solution of C-Dots in triply-distilled water (60 g mL−1) was prepared at pH 2.0 (using a universal buffer) and ionic strength of 0.5 mol L−1 using NaCl salt. The fluorescence intensity of the C-Dots solution was measured at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm and a maximum emission wavelength of 465 nm. As the interaction between NO2 − and C-Dots caused the quenching effect in the fluorescence intensity, this phenomenon was selected as a selective probe for the detection and determination of nitrite even in the presence of nitrate. Determination of both nitrite and nitrate species was achieved by the reduction of NO3 − to NO2 − during its selective reduction using a cadmium powder. In spite of the toxicity of the cadmium powder, but reduction potential of cadmium is enough for rapid reduction of NO3 − to NO2 −. Also compared to other reducing agent such as BH4-, that would reduce NO3 − into N2, cadmium is acceptable. Regarding the results reported in Ref. [20], chemical reduction of nitrate to nitrite would be done in presence of cadmium metal. For this purpose, 125.00 mg cadmium powder was prepared by rasping into the cadmium bullion and placing in a fluorescent cuvette contacting during stirring with a small magnet for 8 min. The fluorescent intensity was then attributed to the quenching of the NO3 − solution. Therefore, the quenched value was used as a selective probe of NO3 − detection and determination.

In this study NOx gas solutions were generated via reaction between copper and nitric acid according to the instrumentation and chemical equation shown in Fig. 1 . For this purpose, a fixed amount of copper (analytical grade, Merck) ranging between 1.00 and 10.00 mg (depending on the NOx solution) was mixed with excess amount (20.0 mL) of commercial HNO3 (purity: 65.0%, Kimia Mavad Company, Iran) inside a 100.0-mL balloon as closed trap. After formation of NOx during about 5.0 min time interval, the generated NOx gas was transferred into a cell containing 2.0 mL aqueous solution of C-Dots (80.0 g mL−1) by the flow of N2 as carrier gas for 5.0 s. The time interval of purging NO2 was controlled using an electronic valve by computer through an electronic circuit. However large fractions of the synthesized gas were NO2 gas, but due to the instability of this gas the detection was reported based on the NOx gases.

For determination of NOx, the fluorescence of the C-Dots was measured, at first. After that, the gas solution with an appropriate concentration of NOX gas was blown into the solution and the fluorescence intensity was estimated during the decrease in the fluorescence intensity. All experiments were performed at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, the emission wavelength of 460 nm and pH 2.0 as an optimum pH using the universal buffer solution (0.2 mol L−1). For each experiment, 80 g mL−1 C-Dots as optimum concentration was used. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at different time intervals to optimize the incubation time. The Effect of probable interferences of certain foreign species such as CO2, CH4, CO, n-hexane and gasoline vapor was also evaluated in detail during spiking into a standard solution at pH 2.0. The configuration has been shown in Fig. 1.

In this study the analytical aspects of a novel N-CD was investigated in detail. The synthesized N-CD due to the possessing various characteristics such as nano-size dimension, presence of nitrogen functional groups on its matrix, etc. has been considered as an acceptable probe for the fabrication of different types of optical sensors for ultra-trace detection purposes. The optical and structural properties of the synthesized N-CDs were characterized by the ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy and fluorescence spectrometry. In regard with Fig. 2 , the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were observed at 360 and 435 nm values, respectively.
The FT-IR Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was utilized to characterize the N-CDs as shown in Fig. 1A (S.P.).

On the basis of FT-IR spectrum, the N-CDs possessed different absorption bands of triethylenetetramine including 632 as well as 1558, and 3444 cm−1, which were related to the N-H functional group. A wide absorption band at 1184 cm−1 was also attributed to the C-N functional group. These absorption bands were in good agreement with those estimated for these functional groups according to the characterizations reported in Ref. [21], whereas nearly no absorption peak was observed for the synthesized N-CDots. These results indicated that CA might be mostly carbonized during the pyrolysis, as a new sharp peak, associated with amide linkage (–CONH–) at the frequency of 1708 cm−1 [20]. Regarding this evidence, the surface of the C-Dots was probably coated with a large amount of triethylenetetramine through the amide linkages. The XRD pattern (Fig. 1B (S.P.)) was another characterization method. The peaks positioned at 2θ = ∼27, ∼43, and ∼51° were related to C(002), C(100) and C(101), respectively, which were in agreement with the results reported in Ref. [21].

The surface composition and elemental analysis of the resultant dots were characterized by XPS technique as shown in Fig. 2 (S.P.). Regarding the XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 (S.P.)), three independent peaks was apparent at 292.3, 407.4 and 539.1eV (Fig. 4A), which were attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively [1]. The XPS results indicated that the synthesized carbon dots were mainly composed of three elements including C, N, and O. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2B (S.P.)) showed two peaks at 291.2, and 293.4 eV, which were attributed to the C–N, and C=O functional groups, respectively [1]. Moreover, a peak positioned at 405.0 in N1s spectrum (Fig. 4C (S.P.)) was related to N–H groups [21]. While the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D (S.P.)) showed one peak at 537.6 eV that revealed the formation of C=O and/or C–O–C bands [1]. (The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2B (S.P.)) showed two peaks at 291.2, and 293.4 eV, which were attributed to the C–N, and C=O functional groups, respectively [1], While the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D (S.P.)) showed one peak at 537.6 eV that revealed the formation of C=O and/or C–O–C bands [1]. A peak positioned at 405.0 in N1s spectrum (Fig. 2C (S.P.)) was also related to N–H groups [21].) The above observations confirmed that the synthesized C-Dots were functioned with amino, hydroxyl, and carboxylic/carbonyl moieties which were origin from precursors [1]. The HRSEM (Fig. 3 A) and HRTEM (Fig. 3B) images depicted the size distribution from 7 to 11 nm. This result was in good agreement with that of particle size analyzer (PZA, model: SALD-301V, Shimadzu) as shown in Fig. 3C.

The elemental analysis was also done using the energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDAX). On the basis of the EDAX elemental analysis, the synthesized N-CDs were at most made of more than 58% carbon atom, 17% nitrogen atom, and 23% oxygen. Moreover, the photographic images have been shown in Fig. 3 (S.P.), which visually shows the fluorescence intensity of synthesized C-Dots.

Important parameters affecting the final photoluminescence of the C-Dots were investigated. These parameters included the source of nitrogen atom doped into the C-Dots matrix, ratio of carbon to nitrogen sources, excitation wavelength, power and time duration of microwave radiation, and also the pH of the C-Dots-containing solution.

In this study, triethylenetetraamine was selected as the nitrogen source. This selection was based on the high solubility of reagent in the aqueous medium, large percentage of nitrogen atoms in this molecule, and finally high nucleophilic activity of the nitrogen atoms (1st and 2nd types of amine). In addition, the citric acid because of possessing significant characteristics including the high chemical reactivity due to three acidic, and one hydroxyl functional groups as well as the quasi-spherical shape was able to react through these functional groups. (In addition, Citric acid was able to react through these functional groups, because of possessing significant characteristics such as high chemical activity due to three acidic, and one hydroxyl functional groups as well as the quasi-spherical shape.) Therefore, this reagent was chosen as the starting material (carbon source).

To optimize the weight ratio of carbon source to the nitrogen source, fixed amount of the triethylenetetramine (nitrogen source) was physically added with different concentrations of CA as the carbon source and also the fluorescence of the products was monitored at 450 nm. On the basis of the fluorescence intensity at the maximum value of wavelength, the ratio of 15.6 mmol (3.0 g) CA per each 3.25 mmol (0.50 mL) triethylenetetraamine was selected as an optimum value.

The carbonization process was done by the use of a domestic microwave. The selection of microwave was due to the homogenization of the microwave radiation during the carbonization process, Therefore, 680-w power and the 50-min time duration of microwave irradiation were used as optimum values.

The temperature of the solution of synthesized C-Dots was studied. As anticipated, fluorescence intensity decreased during the growth in temperature. Since when the C-Dots were in the excited state during the irradiation of UV light, a fraction of them would relax through the self-absorbing or collisional deactivation process. This process occurred more at higher temperatures, due to the plenty of collisions at high temperatures.

The excitation wavelength was also dependent on the concentration of the C-Dots solution. The optimum excitation wavelength was estimated to be 350 nm at C-Dot concentrations lower than ∼200 μgm L−1, whereas for higher C-Dot concentrations, two different excitation wavelengths were estimated. These wavelengths were 324 and 400 nm. It seemed that this phenomenon was because of the self-absorption process.

Due to the dependency of the fluorescent intensity of the synthesized C-Dots to the pH (perhaps because of interaction between nitrogen functional groups and H+), the pH value of the solution was considered as another important factor during the evaluation of maximum fluorescence intensity of synthesized C-Dots. For this purpose, analytical grades of HCl and NaOH solution (concentrated) have been adapted to control the pH of the solution ranging between 2 and 12 at 350 nm excitation wavelength. The results have been shown in Fig. 4. According to the results, the highest intensity was observed at the pH value equal to 6.0, which this value was selected as an optimum pH.
wavelength of 350 nm and room temperature, inset) histogram.

To estimate the reproducibility, five replicate solutions of C-Dots were prepared and the fluorescence intensities were checked. A small difference was observed between the fluorescence intensities of the synthesized C-Dots. The relative standard deviation (RSD %, n = 5) was estimated at 2.77%. The stability of the C-Dots was also evaluated during the estimation of the fluorescence intensity on different days. The RSD% for the synthesized C-Dots was estimated at 5.26% during 29 days in a dark vial at the room temperature. No significant changes were observed in the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized C-Dots up to about 12 days. About 16.12% reduction was observed in the fluorescence intensity after 29th day.

The quantum yield is calculated using Eq. (1) [22].
(1)
Q =
Q R
(
I/I R
)
(
A R
/A )
(
n 2
/n 2
R )
where I was the integrated fluorescence intensity, N was the refractive index, R referred to the reference fluorophore (quinine sulfate) of a known quantum yield and A was the absorption. As results showed, the QY was equal to ∼0.395.

In this study effect of different types of metal ions as foreign species was also investigated in detail. Clearly those of metal ions such as Fe3+ that played role as lewis acid, directly affected pH of the solution and consequently quenched the fluorescence intensity of N-CD. For further evaluation of the probable interfering effect of these metal species or its influence on the pH value of the solution, the effect of these foreign species was evaluated under buffer condition using universal buffer. Fortunately no significant changes were observed in the fluorescence intensity under buffer condition. This phenomenon pointed to the lack of interfering effect of metal species during the pH measurement.

As the fluorescence intensity of synthesized N-CDs was dependent on the pH, it could also be used for constructing an optical pH sensor. The PH dependency of this C-Dot has been shown in the Fig. 5 . As it has been clearly shown, the C-Dots were applicable as an optical pH sensor in the range of 7.0–10.0 (Fig. 5A) as well as 2.0–5.0 (Fig. 5B). In addition, the C-Dots were capable of determining the pH in nonaqueous solutions according to the results shown in Fig. 5C. Since unfortunately, there was no reliable standard method to calibrate the signals of this method, the weight percentage of HClO4 (as the acid in citric acid) was determined as the order of acidity.

For studying the reversibility of introduced pH sensor, the pH of C-Dots was increased from 1.5 to 5.0 (by addition of NaOH) and then reduced to 1.5 (by addition of HCl). The fluorescence intensity was monitored to study the reversibility. The results have been shown in Fig. 4 (S.P.). Reversible responses were therefore estimated for the fabricated pH-based probe.

No significant change was observed in the fluorescence intensity during several and sequentially pH measurements. Also no major hysteresis was observed during providing strong acidic and basic conditions for the N-CDs. Consequently reversibly results were evaluated without need to any extra reagent.

To construct the calibration curve for pH sensing, the C-Dots solutions with different pH values in the range of 1.5–12.0 were prepared (by use of potentiometric pH meter as standard method). The fluorescence intensities of all solutions were then recorded (insets of Fig. 5). Two dynamic ranges were obtained with the pH ranges of 1.5–5.0 and 7.0–10.0. And for the calibration curve in nonaqueous solvents, acetic acid was utilized as a solvent and HClO4 was utilized as an acid to reduce the pH of the solution. The quantity of HClO4 was determined as the degree of the solution acidity (Fig. 5C). Consequently, this probe can be selected as a reliable and sensitive analytical technique for the estimation of acidity and basicity in the nonaqueous media.

Two independent aqueous solutions were used as real samples. The analyzed samples were adopted to approve the ability of the synthesized C-Dots during the determination of the pH values. Analyzing the real sample for an aqueous solution was done by a thirty-time dilution and using the standard addition method. The real sample in aqueous solution was analyzed by assisting the pH sensitive glass electrode, as a reference method, and compared to the reported method. The samples were also employed to detect the acidity of an acetic acid solution as a nonaqueous real sample in the presence of different percentages of HClO4 as an analyte. Real sample analysis was done for a nonaqueous solution by spiking known values of the analyte during the estimation of recovery percentages after 20-times dilution. The results were shown in Tables 1 and 2
. As results show, this method was considered as a reliable technique for accurate and precise detection of pH in both aqueous and nonaqueous media.

For determination of nitrite (NO2 −), the initial fluorescence intensity of C-Dots was measured. Then 10.0 μL NaNO2 stock solution (0.01 mol L−1) was injected and the fluorescence intensity was followed up. Decreasing in the intensity was related to the concentration of nitrite (Fig. 6 A).

Reduction in the fluorescence intensity was probably attributed to the formation of azo dye during the interaction between NO2 − and the N-doped C-Dots at the acidic pH (2.0). And for nitrate (NO3 −), the detection process for nitrate (NO3 −) was the same as nitrite, except the process that happened in the presence of cadmium metal powder as the reducing agent. Therefore, it was possible to simultaneously determine both NO2 − and NO3 − by the recommended procedure. The formation of diazonium salt was probably confirmed via observation of green color during the introduction of NO2 − to the N-CD-containing solution in acidic conditions. It also should be taken into account that, due to some limitations such as nano-size and low concentration of N-CDs as well as the presence of some optical interference in the IR spectrometry, it was impossible to characterize the formation of diazonium salts with spectroscopic technique.

All the parameters which affected the sensitivity and limitation of determination of NO2 − and NO3 − included: pH, temperature, ionic strength, time of stirring and the amount of cadmium metal (for reduction of nitrate to nitrite). One-at-a-time method was adopted for the optimization process.

The pH 2.0 was selected as the optimum value since the fluorescence intensity was partially lowered by increasing the alkalinity. It should be mentioned that by increasing the pH, the C-Dots surface had a negative charge and consequently the repulsion forces between NO2 − and C-Dots prevented the quenching process. The corresponding results were shown in Fig. 6B. The interaction of C-Dots and NO2 − ion flourished during increasing the temperature and as a consequence, the quench of fluorescence was promoted. However, the difference between intensities in different temperatures was not promoted too much. Therefore, the room temperature (∼22 °C) was selected to make the detection process simple. Ionic strength was optimized and the ionic strength of 0.5 mol L−1 was chosen as an optimum value.

The incubation period which the C-Dots and analyte were in contact with each other was also an important parameter. It was clear that, by increasing this factor, higher fluorescence sensitivity was estimated for this probe during the detection of NO3 − and NO2 − ions. During measuring the fluorescence intensity at different time periods, the maximum sensitivity was observed for 6.0 and 8.0 min for NO2 − and NO3 −, respectively. Consequently, 6.0 min was selected for NO2 − and 8.0 min was chosen for NO3 − as the optimum stirring time.

For optimizing the amount of cadmium powder, it was clear that by increasing the amount of cadmium powder, the reduction of nitrate to nitrite would become faster and easier. Therefore, there was a reversible relationship between the amount of cadmium (Cd) and the reduction time. On the basis of analyses, the maximum conversion was observed during using 125.0 mg Cd. Consequently, 125.0 mg was selected as the optimum value for cadmium amount, And finally the concentration of 60.0 μgmL−1 was selected as the optimum concentration of C-Dots.

Generally, to determine the fluorescence restoring or quenching mechanisms, the measurements must be performed at different temperatures. It was known that the mechanism would be dynamic if the slope of fluorescence variation versus temperature was positive; otherwise, the mechanism was static [22]. According to the positive slopes observed for the correlated lines in Fig. 6C, it was concluded that quenching fluorescence process followed a dynamic process. Dynamic quenching or collisional quenching occurred when the excited state fluorophores (i.e. C-Dots) was deactivated by other molecules (i.e. NO2 −) in solution. The Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (2)) can be used for this case [22].
(2)
F 0
/F =
1 + K
SV
[ Q ]
where K
SV was the Stern-Volmer quenching constant indicating the sensitivity of the fluorophore to a quencher. The F
0 was the bare fluorescence in the absence of quencher and F was the quenched fluorescence of C-Dots. Also, the [Q] was the concentration of quencher.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration curve and figures of merit
ce:section.ce:para: The calibration curves for NO2 − and NO3 − have been illustrated in Fig. 7 . The sensitivity of the sensor for the determination of low concentrations of NO2 − and NO3 − was evaluated according to the calibration curves shown in the insets of Fig. 7. As shown, the fluorescence sensor was linear with the linear ranges from 1.0 × 10−7 –to 7.5 × 10−5 and from 2.5 × 10−6 –to 7.5 × 10−4 mol L−1 for NO2 − and NO3 − determinations, respectively. The correlation coefficients (R2) of the calibration curves were the representative of linearity characteristic, which for NO2 − and NO3 − were estimated to be 0.997 and 0.994, respectively.

The calibration sensitivity of the method was evaluated according to the slope of the calibration curves (Fig. 7). These values were estimated to be 89.26 (a.u.) M−1 for NO2 − and 89.77 (a.u.) M−1 for NO3 −. The linear range for nitrite and nitrate were from 1.00 × 10−7 to 7.50 × 10−5 mol L−1 and from 2.50 × 10−6 to 7.50 × 10−4 mol L−1, respectively. The detection limit was also defined as the concentration of the analyte, which was equal to the triple standard deviation of averages obtained from seven to eleven series of recorded fluorescence intensities. On the basis of this definition, the limit of detection was found as 2.50 × 10−8 mol L−1 for NO2 − and 7.50 × 10−7 mol L−1 for NO3 −. The relative standard deviation (repeatability, %RSD) was estimated to be 3.69% (n = 5) for NO2 − and 3.54% (n = 5) for NO3 −. According to the 5 replicate analyses, the reproducibility (%RSD) was estimated at +7.60%, for NO2 − and +10.40% for NO3 −.

The selectivity of the proposed method was tested by studying the effects of some foreign species on the determination of 5.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 NO2 − at the optimum experimental conditions. As mentioned in detail, possible quenching for C-Dots fluorescence was not observed in the presence of these foreign species. Thirteen different ionic species were spiked into the analyte solution, and the probable interfering behavior was examined. The results have been shown in Fig. 8 .

The tested foreign species did not cause any quenching process in fluorescence intensity. In another word, these values (Fig. 8) were the highest threshold (maximum fold-execs) of the tested foreign species without providing any interfering effect during the determination of NO2 − and NO3 −. For the values higher than these thresholds, the positive error was observed depending on the fold excess of the coexisting species. This result, subsequently implied the selectivity of this method for the detection of NO2 − and NO3 −, in the presence of at least 70-fold excess of coexisting species in the real samples.

For analyzing the real samples, each real sample was diluted 30 times and analyzed by standard addition method. The reliability of the results was checked by the use of “Griess” standard method for simultaneous detection of both NO2 − and NO3 − ions [23]. The results were shown in Tables 3 and 4
for NO2 − and NO3 − during analyzing using the standard addition method. Consequently, reliable and valid results were estimated for the introduced probe.

For determination of NOX gas, the initial intensity of C-Dots was measured. Then the gaseous solution of NOX was blown and the fluorescence intensity was followed up. The reduction of the intensity was therefore related to the concentration of NOX (Fig. 9 A and B). Because of in-availability of the NO2 gas cylinder as well as non-reliability of current commercial NOx sensors such as MQ-3 sensors [24], the NOX gas was generated via the reaction between copper powder and nitric acid (Eq. (3)).
(3)
Cu
( s )
+
4 HNO
3
( aq )
→
Cu
(
NO 3
)
2
( aq )
+
2 NO
2
( g )
+
2 H
2
O
( l )

Based on this equation, copper metal was selected as excess reagent. Therefore the amount of NOx was determined based on the stoichiometric coefficient between NO2 as the product of the reaction and HNO3 as the limiting reagent. The quantity of the formation of NOx was also evaluated by gas chromatography using thermal conductivity detector.

To reach the highest fluorescence sensitivity, some parameters such as pH (2.0), temperature (room temperature), ionic strength (1.0 and 0.3 mol L−1) and C-Dots concentration (70.0 mgL−1) were optimized. One-at-a-time method was employed for the optimization process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration curve and figures of merit
ce:section.ce:para: Two calibration curves were obtained, one in presence of 1.0 mol L−1 NaCl and the other during the introduction of 0.3 mol L−1 NaCl. The calibration curves for NOX gas was illustrated in Fig. 9C and 9D. (0.3 mol L−1 NaCl) and 21 (1.0 mol L−1 NaCl). The correlation coefficients (R2) of the calibration curves were estimated to be 0.982 for the calibration at 1.0 M ionic strength and 0.985 (in presence of 0.3 mol L−1 NaCl).

According to Fig. 9 , two linear dynamic ranges were obtained ranging from 3.77 to 36.51 parts per thousand (ppt, 0.37–3.65%) and from 27.67 to 43.77 ppt (2.77–4.38%), respectively in the presence of 0.1 and 0.3 mol L−1 NaCl. The detection limit was defined as the concentration of analyte which was equal to triple of the standard deviation of blank divided by slop of calibration curve. With regard to this definition, the limit of detection was found as 1.41 ppt. The repeatability of the sensor was also obtained during several analyses of 25.0 ppt NOX. Relative standard deviation (repeatability, RSD) was 4.37% (n = 5). According to 5 replicate analyses, the reproducibility (%RSD) was estimated to be 7.93%.

In this study, a novel N-CDs was synthesized via the carbonization method. The prominent advantages of this method are its simplicity and rapidity in use as well as its acceptable quantum yield. As an application of the synthesized C-Dots, a novel optical pH sensor was introduced for pH determination in aqueous solutions and also a novel acidity sensor was reported for the estimation of acidity in nonaqueous solutions. Moreover, A new analytical method for determination of NO3 − and NO2 − was introduced via following the quenching behavior of N-doped C-Dots as a novel fluorescence probe with a linear range of 1.0 × 10−7- 7.5 × 10−5 mol L−1 NO2 − and 2.5 × 10−6- 7.5 × 10−4 mol L−1 NO3 −. The comparative table of the introduced methods of synthesizing C-Dots, pH, nitrite and nitrate detection was shown in Table 5 .

It can be concluded that the C-Dots were considered as a suitable fluorescence probe for the determination of NO2 − and NO3 − without any significant interferences. As the final application, a new method was developed for the selective determination of the NOX gasses using C-Dots as a fluorescence probe. The main advantage of this method was its selectivity, rapidity, simplicity and low cost. However, the great advantage of this method was its capability for selective determination of NOX products in industrial scale at ppt levels without having any serious interference.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to acknowledge the support of this work by the Shiraz University Research Council.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2017.02.041.
